prompt:
The `Timer` callback in `lightning.pytorch.callbacks.timer` has a bug: when `duration` is expressed as a `timedelta`, it is compared against wall-clock seconds as if it were already a float, causing premature training stops on long runs.

Before touching the module, map the blast radius:
1. Which modules directly import `lightning.pytorch.callbacks.timer`? List every one by full dotted name.
2. For each direct importer, state how many other modules import it in turn — this is the second-order exposure.
3. Identify the single importer with the highest second-order exposure; it carries the most amplified risk.

Produce the direct-importer list with second-order counts and name the top-risk importer. Do not implement the fix.

A regression in `lightning.pytorch.core.optimizer` causes the learning-rate scheduler step to fire twice per batch when `optimizer_step` is overridden in a `LightningModule`. The fix changes the internal step-counting logic.

Before touching the module, map the blast radius:
1. Which modules directly import `lightning.pytorch.core.optimizer`? Enumerate all of them by full dotted name.
2. For each direct importer, how many modules import it in turn? (second-order exposure)
3. Of the direct importers, name the two with the highest second-order counts — those are the highest-risk files to regression-test.

Produce the full direct-importer list, second-order counts, and the top-2 risk ranking. Do not implement the fix.

A silent bug in `lightning.pytorch.utilities.model_helpers` causes `is_overridden` to return `True` for methods that are inherited but not overridden, masking callback dispatch errors in certain model hierarchies.

Before fixing this, understand the full blast radius:
1. List every module that directly imports `lightning.pytorch.utilities.model_helpers` by full dotted name.
2. Group the importers into three buckets: trainer-core, callbacks, and everything else.
3. Among all importers, which single module is itself imported by the most other modules? That module concentrates the highest cascading risk.

Produce the full importer list, the three-bucket grouping, and the highest-centrality importer. Do not implement the fix.

A breaking change to the exception hierarchy in `lightning.pytorch.utilities.exceptions` — specifically renaming `MisconfigurationException` to `LightningConfigError` — is being evaluated for the next major release.

Before any rename, assess the true scope:
1. List every module that directly imports `lightning.pytorch.utilities.exceptions` by full dotted name — this is the direct blast radius.
2. Of those direct importers, which are themselves imported by 10 or more modules? These are the high-centrality intermediaries that amplify the blast radius into a second wave.
3. Estimate the total number of unique modules affected if the rename propagates through the high-centrality intermediaries (direct importers + their rdep sets — deduplicated). You may approximate based on the rdep counts you discover.

Produce the direct importer list, the high-centrality intermediary list with their rdep counts, and the estimated total affected module count. Do not implement any rename.

We want to add a `freeze_until_epoch` parameter to `lightning.pytorch.callbacks.finetuning` so users can defer layer unfreezing without subclassing.

Before implementing, scope the change:
1. Which modules import `lightning.pytorch.callbacks.finetuning`? List them all by full dotted name.
2. For each importer, state whether it is part of the public API surface (i.e., re-exports or documents the class) or an internal consumer. Classify as `public` or `internal`.
3. Which importer would require the most careful update because it is itself most widely imported?

Produce the importer list with public/internal classification and identify the highest-risk importer. Do not implement the feature.

We are adding a `map_location` parameter to the load helpers in `lightning.fabric.utilities.load` so that checkpoints can be loaded directly to a target device without a separate `.to()` call.

Before writing any code, assess the integration surface:
1. List every module that imports `lightning.fabric.utilities.load` by full dotted name — these are the callers that will gain access to the new parameter.
2. Of those callers, which two have the most downstream importers of their own? Those two carry the highest amplification risk if their call sites change.
3. Does `lightning.pytorch.trainer.trainer` depend on `lightning.fabric.utilities.load` directly or through one or more intermediaries? Trace the chain.

Produce the caller list, the top-2 high-amplification callers, and the dependency chain (or 'not reachable'). Do not implement the feature.

We want to add a `group` parameter to `lightning.fabric.utilities.rank_zero`'s logging utilities so that logging can be restricted to a specific process group instead of always defaulting to rank 0 of the world group.

Before writing any code, assess scope and coupling:
1. Enumerate every module that imports `lightning.fabric.utilities.rank_zero` by full dotted name. This is your change surface.
2. Among those importers, which modules also import `lightning.pytorch.utilities.rank_zero`? Modules on both lists call both rank-zero implementations and represent the highest consistency risk — the new parameter must be mirrored or a divergence will be introduced.
3. Of the full importer list, rank the top 3 by their own importer count (highest rdep_count first).

Produce the full importer list, the dual-importer overlap list, and the top-3 by centrality. Do not implement the feature.

We plan to add a new `ReduceOp` protocol type to `lightning.fabric.utilities.types` and deprecate the direct use of `torch.distributed.ReduceOp` throughout the codebase, replacing it with the new protocol.

Before touching anything, map the introduction surface:
1. List all modules that import `lightning.fabric.utilities.types` by full dotted name. These modules will gain access to the new protocol type and are the primary propagation surface.
2. Among those importers, which are part of the `lightning.pytorch` namespace? They indicate cross-namespace coupling and will require coordinated updates in both fabric and pytorch layers.
3. Which five importers have the highest rdep counts of their own? Adding the new type to those modules amplifies exposure the most.
4. Report the total importer count.

Produce the full importer list, the cross-namespace list, the top-5 by centrality, and the total count. Do not implement the deprecation.

We are refactoring `lightning.pytorch.callbacks.lr_finder` to extract its internal `_lr_find` helper into a standalone function so it can be called without instantiating the callback.

Before making any changes:
1. Which modules import `lightning.pytorch.callbacks.lr_finder`? List them by full dotted name — they are the only callers affected.
2. For each importer, does it reference `_lr_find` directly (and therefore breaks if the helper is removed), or does it only instantiate the callback class? Classify each as `helper-dependent` or `class-only`.
3. Rank the importers by risk: `helper-dependent` callers must be updated before the refactor can land.

Produce the importer list with dependency classification and risk ranking. Do not implement the refactor.

We are refactoring `lightning.fabric.plugins.environments.cluster_environment` — specifically renaming the `creates_processes_externally` property to `is_interactive_compatible` to align with a new cross-framework naming convention.

Before touching the file:
1. Enumerate every module that imports `lightning.fabric.plugins.environments.cluster_environment` by full dotted name. Each one must be audited for use of the old property name.
2. For each importer, how many modules import it in turn? The importers with high second-order counts carry the most cascading rename risk.
3. Name the top 2 importers by second-order count — those two should be prioritised in the rename sweep.

Produce the full importer list with second-order counts, and identify the top-2 priority targets. Do not implement the rename.

We are refactoring `lightning.fabric.utilities.distributed` to replace its ad-hoc `barrier` wrappers with a unified `DistributedBarrier` context manager, which changes the call interface for every consumer.

Before touching the file:
1. List every module that imports `lightning.fabric.utilities.distributed` by full dotted name. This is your mandatory update list.
2. Of those importers, which five have the most downstream importers of their own? Changing those five modules carries the largest cascading risk and should be scheduled last in the refactor sequence.
3. Does the refactor need to extend into the `lightning.pytorch` namespace? Identify any importers that are themselves in the `lightning.pytorch` package.

Produce the full importer list, the top-5 by centrality in order, and the cross-namespace list. Do not implement the refactor.

We are refactoring `lightning.pytorch.callbacks` — the package `__init__` — to split its public exports into two sub-modules: `lightning.pytorch.callbacks.training` and `lightning.pytorch.callbacks.evaluation`, with the top-level `__init__` becoming a thin re-export shim.

Before restructuring anything:
1. List every module that imports `lightning.pytorch.callbacks` by full dotted name. Any module that imports from this namespace is affected by the export split.
2. Of those importers, which modules are in the `tests` namespace? Exclude them — test modules are not part of the production blast radius. Report how many test-namespace importers were excluded.
3. Among the remaining production importers, which 10 have the highest rdep counts of their own? These are the highest-amplification paths and must be verified after the shim is in place.
4. Report the total production importer count (excluding tests).

Produce the production importer list, excluded test count, top-10 by centrality, and total production count. Do not implement the refactor.

A PR modifies `lightning.pytorch.strategies.deepspeed` to add support for ZeRO stage 3 with CPU offloading, touching the `__init__` signature and internal setup hooks.

Review the risk before approving:
1. Which modules import `lightning.pytorch.strategies.deepspeed`? List them by full dotted name — each is a direct blast-radius target.
2. For each direct importer, report its own rdep count. An importer with many downstream consumers amplifies the regression surface.
3. Is this change isolated (all importers have rdep count ≤ 5) or does it reach widely-imported modules? State your conclusion.

Produce the importer list with rdep counts and the isolation verdict. Do not approve or reject the PR.

A PR proposes changing the signature of the `_convert_fp_tensor` helper in `lightning.fabric.plugins.precision.utils`, replacing the `dtype` positional argument with a keyword-only argument to prevent silent argument-order bugs.

Quantify the risk before approving:
1. List every module that imports `lightning.fabric.plugins.precision.utils` by full dotted name. Each one is a potential call-site that must be updated.
2. How many of those importers also import other modules from `lightning.fabric.plugins.precision.*`? Modules that couple to multiple precision utilities are the most fragile — list them.
3. Of all direct importers, which three have the highest rdep counts? Those carry the most downstream regression risk.

Produce the full importer list, the multi-precision-coupled list, and the top-3 by centrality. Do not approve or reject the PR.

A PR removes three deprecated symbols from `lightning.pytorch.utilities` (the package `__init__`): `rank_zero_deprecation`, `AMPType`, and `GradClipAlgorithmType`. The symbols have been in the deprecated state for two major versions.

Before approving, assess the true removal risk:
1. List every module that imports `lightning.pytorch.utilities` by full dotted name. These are all candidates for using the removed symbols.
2. Among those importers, which also import `lightning.pytorch.utilities.rank_zero` or `lightning.pytorch.utilities.enums` directly? Those modules may have already migrated to the canonical import path and are lower risk — flag them as `migrated-candidate`.
3. Rank the remaining importers (not flagged as migrated) by their own rdep counts, highest first — these are the highest regression risk if any of the three symbols are still in use.

Produce the full importer list, the migrated-candidate list, and the non-migrated importers ranked by centrality. Do not approve or reject the PR.

A PR replaces the `rank_zero_warn` function in `lightning.pytorch.utilities.rank_zero` with a new `rank_zero_warn_once` implementation that deduplicates repeated warnings, changing the function signature to add an optional `key` parameter.

Before approving, conduct a full risk assessment:
1. List all modules that import `lightning.pytorch.utilities.rank_zero` by full dotted name. This is the direct exposure surface.
2. Which of those importers are themselves imported by 10 or more modules? These are the high-centrality intermediaries — any breakage in them cascades widely.
3. Among the full importer set, how many are in the `tests` namespace versus production? Report both counts separately.
4. Considering the breadth of this change (64 direct importers, high-centrality intermediaries, cross-namespace reach), assign a risk tier: `low` / `medium` / `high` / `critical`, and justify your classification using the numbers you discovered.

Produce the full importer list, high-centrality intermediaries, test vs production counts, and the risk-tier verdict with justification. Do not approve or reject the PR.
